title: Golubtsy
subtitle: Russian stuffed cabbage rolls in tomato sauce
excerpt: White cabbage leaves wrapped around a filling of minced pork and rice, simmered in a rich tomato and onion sauce. Russia's version of the stuffed-cabbage family, braised slowly until the leaves are tender and the sauce thickens around the rolls.
sourceNotes: Based on the golubtsy tradition as documented across Russian and Soviet-era cookery literature, including references in Elena Molokhovets, 'A Gift to Young Housewives' (1861, public domain) and Anya von Bremzen and John Welchman, 'Please to the Table' (1990). Stuffed cabbage appears in nearly every Eastern European cuisine; the Russian version is distinguished by its tomato-onion sauce and the combination of pork and beef mince.

content.text: Golubtsy means 'little pigeons' in Russian, a term of affection for these small parcels of cabbage. They are braised in tomato and onion sauce until the filling is cooked through and the cabbage softens from a leaf into something closer to a second skin around the mince. The sauce simmers down to a deep, concentrated thickness, sour from the tomato and sweet from the long-cooked onion.

The tricky part is blanching the cabbage to make it flexible enough to roll without tearing. Two minutes in boiling water is enough for the outer leaves; the inner ones need less. Peel the leaves gently; they come away cleanly once the head has been blanched twice.
The rice in the filling goes in raw and half-cooked. It finishes cooking in the roll during braising and expands to fill the parcel. Don't use pre-cooked rice; it will become mushy by the end.

Cut the core out of the base of {{cabbage-white}} head of cabbage. Lower the whole head into a large pan of boiling salted water for 3 minutes. Lift out, peel away the outer leaves, and return the head to the water. Repeat until you have 12 large leaves. Cut away the thick central rib of each leaf with a small knife, working from the base upwards so the leaf stays intact.

Combine {{pork-mince}} of pork mince, {{beef-mince}} of beef mince, {{long-grain-rice}} of raw rinsed rice, {{onion}} grated onion, {{garlic}} of crushed garlic, salt, and pepper in a bowl. Mix until uniform. Place 2 to 3 tablespoons of filling near the base of each leaf. Fold the sides in, then roll up firmly from the base. The rice will expand; don't pack too tightly.

Soften {{onion}} sliced onions in oil in a wide casserole for 10 minutes. Add {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée, {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes, and {{stock-beef}} of beef stock. Season. Lay the rolls seam-side down in the sauce in a single layer, packing them snugly so they don't unroll. Bring to a simmer, cover, and cook on the lowest heat for 55 to 65 minutes until the rice is cooked through and the cabbage is completely tender.

items.fix: Pack the rolls tightly into the casserole so they hold each other in place. Use a slightly smaller dish if needed.
items.cause: Not packed snugly enough, or filling overstuffed

items.fix: Add more stock or water, cover, and continue braising. The rice needs liquid to cook through.
items.cause: Sauce has reduced so the rolls are no longer submerged in liquid

items.fix: Blanch for 3 minutes per batch. Trim the rib more aggressively, a leaf that lies flat rolls cleanly.
items.cause: Leaves not blanched long enough or central rib not trimmed

items.fix: Uncover for the last 15 minutes and turn up the heat slightly to reduce the sauce.
items.cause: Lid too tight keeping moisture in

content.text: Stuffed cabbage appears across a huge swathe of Central and Eastern Europe. The Polish gołąbki, Hungarian töltött káposzta, Ukrainian holubtsi, and Romanian sarmale are all distinct in filling and seasoning. They share the same basic form. The Russian golubtsy is braised in tomato sauce with a pork-and-beef mince filling. It is the version most known in English-speaking countries. At home in Russia, this is Sunday food: takes time, keeps well, and tastes better the next day.
